preamble.summary: Elihu's second speech addresses a wider audience — he calls upon the wise men to listen and judge between his words and Job's. He quotes Job's claim that he is righteous but denied justice, then methodically argues that God cannot be unjust. His argument is structural, not evidential: God governs the entire earth; if God withdrew his spirit, all flesh would perish. Therefore God cannot act with partiality or injustice, because the one who holds all life in his hands has no motive for corruption. God does not need to examine anyone at length — he knows their works and overturns them in a night. He strikes the wicked in open sight, because they turned aside from following him and caused the cry of the poor to reach his ears. When God is silent, who can condemn? When he hides his face, who can see him? Yet he rules over nations and individuals alike. Elihu concludes by calling on Job to submit: if Job has sinned without knowing it, let God teach him; if Job has done wrong, let him repent. The decision is Job's, not Elihu's.
preamble.remarkable: Elihu's argument in this chapter operates at a different level than the friends' arguments. The friends reasoned backward from suffering to guilt: you suffer, therefore you sinned. Elihu reasons forward from God's nature to God's justice: God governs all things, therefore God cannot be unjust. This is a cosmological argument, not a forensic one. The most powerful section (vv. 14-15) imagines what would happen if God withdrew his spirit from the world — all flesh would expire, all humanity would return to dust. This counterfactual proves God's ongoing sustaining will: the fact that anything exists at all demonstrates that God is actively choosing to sustain life. A God who sustains everything by choice cannot be accused of indifferent injustice. The argument has force, but it also has a blind spot: it proves that God can do justice but does not explain why God's justice sometimes looks like injustice from the human side. Elihu knows this — his conclusion (vv. 31-37) shifts from argumentation to exhortation, urging Job to let God be the teacher rather than the defendant.
preamble.friction: Elihu's quotation of Job in verses 5-6 is more pointed than his previous summary: he now attributes to Job the claim that 'God has taken away my justice' and 'my wound is incurable though I am without transgression.' These are fair paraphrases of Job's actual words (27:2, 6:4, 9:17). But Elihu then adds a charge that is more aggressive: 'What man is like Job, who drinks scorn like water, who walks in company with evildoers?' (vv. 7-8). This is not something Job said — it is Elihu's characterization of the effect of Job's words. By claiming innocence while accusing God, Job has, in Elihu's view, aligned himself with those who mock God. This is a harder edge than Elihu showed in chapter 33, and it raises the question of whether Elihu is beginning to slide into the same condemnation that defeated the three friends. The chapter oscillates between genuine theological insight (vv. 14-15) and rhetorical aggression (vv. 7-8, 36-37), making Elihu a more complex and less sympathetic figure than in his first speech.
preamble.connections: The counterfactual of verses 14-15 (if God withdrew his spirit, all would die) connects to Psalm 104:29-30 ('when you take away their breath, they die and return to dust; when you send forth your spirit, they are created') and to Genesis 2:7 / 3:19 (from dust, to dust). Elihu's assertion that God shows no partiality to princes (v. 19) echoes Deuteronomy 10:17 ('the LORD your God is God of gods, who shows no partiality and accepts no bribe'). The image of God overturning the mighty in a night (v. 25) anticipates Daniel's theology of divine sovereignty over kingdoms (Daniel 2:21, 'he removes kings and sets up kings'). Elihu's final challenge — 'should God repay according to your terms?' (v. 33) — foreshadows God's own challenge to Job in 38:2 and 40:8.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיַּ֥עַן אֱלִיה֗וּא וַיֹּאמַֽר׃
verses.text_kjv: Furthermore Elihu answered and said,
verses.rendering: Then Elihu answered and said:
translator_notes: The standard speech-introduction formula. Elihu continues without any indication that Job has responded to the invitation of 33:32-33. Job's silence is itself significant — he does not rebut Elihu as he rebutted the three friends.

verses.text_hebrew: שִׁמְע֣וּ חֲכָמִ֣ים מִלָּ֑י וְ֝יֹדְעִ֗ים הַאֲזִ֥ינוּ לִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: Hear my words, O ye wise men; and give ear unto me, ye that have knowledge.
verses.rendering:
Hear my words, you who are wise;
you who have knowledge, listen to me.
translator_notes: Elihu broadens his audience: shim'u chakhamim ('hear, wise ones'). He is no longer addressing Job alone but appealing to a wider circle of sages — possibly the three friends, possibly a broader assembly, possibly a rhetorical audience. The yode'im ('those who know, the knowledgeable') in the parallel line forms a jury: Elihu wants his case judged by competent minds.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּי־אֹ֭זֶן מִלִּ֣ין תִּבְחָ֑ן וְ֝חֵ֗ךְ יִטְעַ֥ם לֶאֱכֹֽל׃
verses.text_kjv: For the ear trieth words, as the mouth tasteth meat.
verses.rendering:
For the ear tests words
as the palate tastes food.
verses.expanded_rendering: Elihu invokes a proverb that Job himself used in 12:11: the ear is the organ of discernment for speech, as the palate is for food. The verb tivchan ('tests, assays, examines') from bachan is the language of metallurgy — testing ore for purity. The ear does not passively receive words; it assays them, weighing their truth-content. The parallel ve-chekh yit'am le-ekhol ('and the palate tastes to eat') makes the same point about food: you do not swallow without tasting. Elihu asks his audience to taste-test his argument before accepting or rejecting it.
translator_notes: This proverb appeared in Job 12:11 in Job's own mouth: 'Does not the ear test words as the palate tastes food?' Elihu either borrows Job's saying or draws from a common wisdom tradition. By using Job's own proverb, Elihu implicitly invites Job to apply his own standard of discernment to what follows.

verses.text_hebrew: מִשְׁפָּ֥ט נִבְחֲרָה־לָּ֑נוּ נֵדְעָ֖ה בֵינֵ֣ינוּ מַה־טּֽוֹב׃
verses.text_kjv: Let us choose to us judgment: let us know among ourselves what is good.
verses.rendering:
Let us choose what is just;
let us determine among ourselves what is good.
translator_notes: The verb nivcharah ('let us choose') from bachar implies a deliberate, considered selection — not impulse but judgment. The mishpat ('justice, judgment, right ruling') is the object of choice: Elihu frames the discussion as a collective search for justice. The ned'ah beinenu mah tov ('let us know among ourselves what is good') invites communal discernment. Elihu positions himself not as a dictator of truth but as a participant in a shared inquiry.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּֽי־אָ֭מַר אִיּ֣וֹב צָדָ֑קְתִּי וְ֝אֵ֗ל הֵסִ֥יר מִשְׁפָּטִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: For Job hath said, I am righteous: and God hath taken away my judgment.
verses.rendering:
For Job has said, 'I am righteous,
but God has taken away my justice.'
translator_notes: Elihu quotes Job's two-part claim: tsadaqti ('I am righteous') and El hesir mishpati ('God has removed my justice'). The first part echoes Job 27:6 ('I hold fast my righteousness and will not let it go'). The second echoes Job 27:2 ('As God lives, who has taken away my justice'). Elihu accurately captures Job's position: I am innocent, and God has denied me the vindication I deserve.

verses.text_hebrew: עַל־מִשְׁפָּטִ֥י אֲכַזֵּ֑ב אָנ֖וּשׁ חִצִּ֣י בְלִי־פָֽשַׁע׃
verses.text_kjv: Should I lie against my right? my wound is incurable without transgression.
verses.rendering:
'Should I lie about my own case?
My wound is incurable, though I am without transgression.'
translator_notes: Elihu continues the quotation. The rhetorical question al mishpati akhazzev ('against my justice should I lie?') paraphrases Job's refusal to recant (27:4-5). The anush chitsi beli fasha ('my arrow-wound is incurable without transgression') captures Job's protest that his suffering (described as an arrow wound, echoing 6:4) has no cause in his behavior. Elihu presents the full force of Job's complaint before attempting to answer it.

verses.text_hebrew: מִי־גֶ֥בֶר כְּאִיּ֑וֹב יִשְׁתֶּה־לַּ֥עַג כַּמָּֽיִם׃
verses.text_kjv: What man is like Job, who drinketh up scorning like water?
verses.rendering:
What man is like Job,
who drinks scorn like water?
verses.expanded_rendering: Elihu's tone sharpens. The rhetorical question mi gever ke-Iyov ('what man is like Job?') is not admiration but accusation. The image: yishteh la'ag ka-mayim ('he drinks mockery like water'). The la'ag ('scorn, mockery, derision') is directed at God — Elihu is saying that Job's persistent complaints about divine injustice amount to gulping down blasphemy as casually as one drinks water. The verb shathah ('to drink') implies habitual consumption — Job has made a habit of scorning God's justice. This is Elihu's harshest characterization yet, and it edges dangerously close to the friends' method of attacking Job's character rather than addressing his arguments.
translator_notes: The image of drinking scorn like water recurs in Job 15:16 (Eliphaz: 'how much less one who drinks injustice like water'). Elihu may be consciously echoing Eliphaz while applying the image differently — not to humanity in general but to Job specifically. The shift from empathetic interlocutor (ch. 33) to accuser marks a tonal change in Elihu's rhetoric.

verses.text_hebrew: וְאָרַ֣ח לְ֭חֶבְרָה עִם־פֹּ֣עֲלֵי אָ֑וֶן וְ֝לָלֶ֗כֶת עִם־אַנְשֵׁי־רֶֽשַׁע׃
verses.text_kjv: Which goeth in company with the workers of iniquity, and walketh with wicked men.
verses.rendering:
He keeps company with workers of evil
and walks alongside wicked men.
translator_notes: The verb arach ('he travels, he journeys') le-chevrah ('for companionship') im po'alei aven ('with workers of iniquity') accuses Job of ideological companionship with the wicked — not that Job commits evil acts, but that his theology aligns him with those who deny God's justice. The parallel ve-lalekhet im anshei resha ('and to walk with men of wickedness') uses the metaphor of walking together, implying shared direction and shared conclusions. Elihu's argument: by saying 'God denies justice,' Job walks the same road as those who reject God entirely.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּֽי־אָ֭מַר לֹ֣א יִסְכׇּן־גָּ֑בֶר בִּ֝רְצֹת֗וֹ עִם־אֱלֹהִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: For he hath said, It profiteth a man nothing that he should delight himself with God.
verses.rendering:
For he has said, 'It profits a person nothing
to take delight in God.'
verses.expanded_rendering: Elihu attributes to Job a conclusion Job never explicitly stated but which Elihu considers the logical end of Job's argument: lo yiskon gaver birtso im Elohim ('it does not profit a man to be pleased with God'). The verb sakan ('to profit, to benefit, to be useful') is a commercial term — Elihu says Job has concluded that friendship with God yields no return on investment. If the righteous suffer like the wicked, what is the advantage of righteousness? This is a fair extrapolation of Job's argument in chapters 9-10 and 21, where Job observed that the wicked prosper while the righteous suffer. But it is also a distortion: Job never said piety is pointless — he said it does not guarantee protection.
translator_notes: The verb ratsah ('to be pleased with, to take delight in') here describes the human side of the relationship: taking pleasure in God, delighting in obedience. Elihu charges that Job has declared this delight worthless. The same question will be raised more subtly by the Adversary's original challenge in 1:9: 'Does Job fear God for nothing?' Elihu, without knowing it, is revisiting the book's foundational question.

verses.text_hebrew: לָ֭כֵן אַנְשֵׁ֣י לֵבָ֑ב שִׁמְע֥וּ לִ֝֗י חָלִ֤לָה לָאֵ֨ל ׀ מֵרֶ֗שַׁע וְשַׁדַּ֥י מֵעָֽוֶל׃
verses.text_kjv: Therefore hearken unto me ye men of understanding: far be it from God, that he should do wickedness; and from the Almighty, that he should commit iniquity.
verses.rendering:
Therefore, you who have understanding, listen to me:
Far be it from God to do wickedness,
far from the Almighty to commit injustice.
verses.expanded_rendering: Elihu's counter-thesis, stated as an axiom: chalilah la-El me-resha ('profanity for God from wickedness' — that is, it is profane even to suggest God does evil). The word chalilah ('far be it, God forbid') is the strongest Hebrew expression of moral impossibility — the very thought is an obscenity. The parallel ve-Shaddai me-avel ('and the Almighty from injustice') extends the claim to the other divine name. Elihu's position is not that God sometimes does justice and sometimes does not — it is that wickedness and God are ontologically incompatible. Injustice is as foreign to God's nature as darkness is to light. This is not an argument from evidence but from the definition of God.
translator_notes: The word chalilah derives from chalal ('to profane, to defile'). To say that God commits evil is to profane God — it violates the sacred nature of divinity itself. Elihu's argument is that Job's accusation is not merely incorrect but sacrilegious. This is stronger language than the friends used, but it rests on a different foundation: the friends accused Job of sin; Elihu accuses Job of theological error.

key_terms.semantic_range: profanation, abomination, 'God forbid,' an expression of moral impossibility or outraged rejection
key_terms.note: chalilah is the Hebrew Bible's strongest way of saying 'this must never be thought, let alone said.' It appears in Genesis 18:25 on Abraham's lips ('far be it from you to do such a thing — to kill the righteous with the wicked') and in 1 Samuel 26:11 ('the LORD forbid that I should put out my hand against the LORD's anointed'). Elihu uses it as the foundation of his entire argument: divine injustice is not merely unlikely but conceptually impossible.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֤י פֹ֣עַל אָ֭דָם יְשַׁלֶּם־ל֑וֹ וּֽכְאֹ֥רַח אִ֝֗ישׁ יַמְצִאֶֽנּוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: For the work of a man shall he render unto him, and cause every man to find according to his ways.
verses.rendering:
For he repays a person according to their deeds
and makes everyone find what their path deserves.
translator_notes: The positive statement of divine justice: fo'al adam yeshallem lo ('a person's work he repays to him'). The verb shillem ('to repay, to complete, to make whole') implies exact correspondence between action and consequence. The parallel u-khe-orach ish yamtsi'ennu ('and according to a person's path he causes him to find') uses the metaphor of a road: the path you walk leads to the destination you reach. Elihu states retribution theology in its simplest form. The question, which Elihu does not fully address, is what to do when the evidence contradicts this principle — as it does in Job's case.

verses.text_hebrew: אַף־אָמְנָ֗ם אֵ֥ל לֹא־יַרְשִׁ֑יעַ וְ֝שַׁדַּ֗י לֹא־יְעַוֵּ֥ת מִשְׁפָּֽט׃
verses.text_kjv: Yea, surely God will not do wickedly, neither will the Almighty pervert judgment.
verses.rendering:
Truly, God does not act wickedly,
and the Almighty does not pervert justice.
translator_notes: The af omnam ('truly indeed, most certainly') is emphatic: El lo yarshi'a ('God does not act wickedly') uses the Hiphil of rasha — God does not cause wickedness, does not practice it, does not produce it. The parallel ve-Shaddai lo ye'avvet mishpat ('and the Almighty does not twist justice') uses the verb ivvet ('to twist, to pervert, to make crooked') — the same verb Bildad used in 8:3. Elihu and Bildad agree on this point, but Elihu will ground it differently: not in tradition but in cosmology.

verses.text_hebrew: מִֽי־פָקַ֣ד עָלָ֣יו אָ֑רְצָה וּמִ֥י שָׂ֝֗ם תֵּבֵ֥ל כֻּלָּֽהּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Who hath given him a charge over the earth? or who hath disposed the whole world?
verses.rendering:
Who entrusted him with the earth?
Who appointed him over the whole world?
verses.expanded_rendering: Elihu shifts to cosmological argument. The question mi faqad alav artsah ('who charged him with the earth?') implies: no one. God was not appointed by a superior authority. God governs the earth by his own sovereign choice, not by delegation. The parallel u-mi sam tevel kullah ('and who set the whole world?') asks the same question about the entire inhabited world. If no one appointed God, then God is not accountable to anyone — but this also means God is not constrained by any external agenda. God's governance is self-motivated, and Elihu will argue that self-motivated governance by a perfect being necessarily produces justice.
translator_notes: The verb paqad ('to appoint, to charge, to visit, to attend to') is rich with meaning — it can mean appointing someone to a task, visiting someone for judgment, or entrusting someone with responsibility. The rhetorical answer to both questions is 'no one' — God is self-appointed, self-authorized, and self-sustained.

verses.text_hebrew: אִם־יָשִׂ֣ים אֵלָ֣יו לִבּ֑וֹ רוּח֥וֹ וְ֝נִשְׁמָת֗וֹ אֵלָ֥יו יֶאֱסֹֽף׃
verses.text_kjv: If he set his heart upon man, if he gather unto himself his spirit and his breath;
verses.rendering:
If God set his heart on it,
if he gathered his spirit and his breath back to himself —
verses.expanded_rendering: The most powerful counterfactual in the Elihu speeches. The im yasim elav libbo ('if he set his heart upon it') — if God decided, if God chose to — rucho venishmato elav ye'esof ('his spirit and his breath he would gather back to himself'). The ruach and neshamah that God breathed into humanity (Genesis 2:7) and that Elihu claimed as his own source of wisdom (32:8, 33:4) — God could recall them. The verb ye'esof ('he would gather') from asaf means to collect, to harvest, to bring home. God could harvest the breath back, recalling it from every living thing. This is not a threat but a demonstration: the fact that God has not done this proves something about God's character. Life itself is evidence of divine grace.
translator_notes: The pairing of ruach and neshamah echoes 33:4 and 32:8, creating a thematic chain: the spirit that gives Elihu authority to speak (32:8), that gives him life (33:4), could be withdrawn at any moment (34:14). Elihu's wisdom, his very existence, depends moment by moment on God's choice to sustain it.

verses.text_hebrew: יִגְוַ֣ע כׇּל־בָּשָׂ֣ר יָ֑חַד וְ֝אָדָ֗ם עַל־עָפָ֥ר יָשֽׁוּב׃
verses.text_kjv: All flesh shall perish together, and man shall turn again unto dust.
verses.rendering:
all flesh would perish together,
and humanity would return to dust.
verses.expanded_rendering: The consequence of divine withdrawal: yigva kol basar yachad ('all flesh would expire together'). The verb gava ('to expire, to breathe the last breath, to die') describes the cessation of breathing — the exact reversal of the creation breath. All flesh — kol basar, the phrase used for every living creature in the flood narrative (Genesis 6:17) — would die simultaneously. The parallel ve-adam al afar yashuv ('and humanity to dust would return') quotes Genesis 3:19 almost verbatim: 'dust you are, and to dust you shall return.' Elihu's counterfactual proves that creation is not a finished event but an ongoing act of divine will. God sustains the world at every moment. The fact that you are alive right now means God is choosing, right now, to keep you alive. A God who actively sustains all life cannot be accused of indifference to justice.
translator_notes: This is arguably the most theologically sophisticated moment in the Elihu speeches. The argument is not 'God is powerful and you should fear him' but 'God actively sustains all life by choice, and the continuation of existence is itself evidence of divine care.' This anticipates the theology of Paul in Acts 17:28 ('in him we live and move and have our being') and Colossians 1:17 ('in him all things hold together').

verses.text_hebrew: וְאִם־בִּ֥ינָה שִׁמְעָה־זֹּ֑את הַ֝אֲזִ֗ינָה לְק֣וֹל מִלָּֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: If now thou hast understanding, hear this: hearken to the voice of my words.
verses.rendering:
If you have understanding, hear this;
listen to the sound of my words.
translator_notes: Elihu pauses for emphasis: ve-im binah ('and if understanding') shim'ah zot ('hear this'). He is about to shift from cosmological argument to political theology — applying the principle of divine justice to the governance of nations and rulers. The ha'azinah le-qol millay ('give ear to the voice of my words') is a formal demand for attention, signaling that a new section of the argument begins.

verses.text_hebrew: הַאַ֬ף שׂוֹנֵ֣א מִשְׁפָּ֣ט יַחֲב֑וֹשׁ וְאִם־צַדִּ֖יק כַּבִּ֣יר תַּרְשִֽׁיעַ׃
verses.text_kjv: Shall even he that hateth right govern? and wilt thou condemn him that is most just?
verses.rendering:
Can one who hates justice govern?
Will you condemn the Righteous and Mighty One?
verses.expanded_rendering: Elihu's question is logical: ha-af sone mishpat yachavosh ('can one who hates justice bind up / rule?'). The verb chavash ('to bind, to bandage, to saddle, to govern') implies authority and control — can someone who despises justice exercise sovereign authority? The implied answer: it is self-contradictory. Governance requires order; order requires justice; a hater of justice cannot govern. The parallel ve-im tsaddiq kabbir tarshi'a ('and will you condemn the Righteous Mighty One?') makes it personal: the tsaddiq kabbir ('the righteous, the mighty') is God, and Job has been condemning him. Elihu's argument: you cannot simultaneously acknowledge God as sovereign and accuse him of injustice, because sovereignty without justice is not sovereignty but chaos.
translator_notes: The word kabbir ('mighty, powerful, great') is rare, appearing mainly in Job. It describes God's overwhelming power combined with righteousness. Elihu's rhetorical question assumes that power and justice are inseparable in God — an assumption Job would challenge by pointing to his own experience.

verses.text_hebrew: הַאֲמֹ֣ר לְ֭מֶלֶךְ בְּלִיָּ֑עַל רָ֝שָׁ֗ע אֶל־נְדִיבִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: Is it fit to say to a king, Thou art wicked? and to princes, Ye are ungodly?
verses.rendering:
Does one say to a king, 'Worthless one!'?
Or to nobles, 'Wicked!'?
translator_notes: Elihu argues from lesser to greater: ha-amor le-melekh beliyya'al ('does one say to a king, worthless?'). Even human kings are not addressed with such contempt. The beliyya'al ('worthless, wicked, useless') is one of the harshest terms in Hebrew — it will later become a name for the ultimate adversary (Belial). If you would not say this to a human king, how much less to the divine King? The rasha el nedivim ('wicked to nobles') extends the point: you do not accuse rulers of wickedness to their faces. Job, in Elihu's view, has done exactly this to God.

verses.text_hebrew: אֲשֶׁ֤ר לֹא־נָשָׂ֨א ׀ פְּנֵ֥י שָׂרִ֗ים וְלֹ֣א נִכַּר־שׁ֭וֹעַ לִפְנֵי־דָ֑ל כִּֽי־מַעֲשֵׂ֖ה יָדָ֣יו כֻּלָּֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: How much less to him that accepteth not the persons of princes, nor regardeth the rich more than the poor? for they all are the work of his hands.
verses.rendering:
How much less the One who shows no favoritism to princes
and does not prefer the rich over the poor —
for they are all the work of his hands.
verses.expanded_rendering: The argument reaches its theological core: asher lo nasa penei sarim ('who does not lift the face of princes') — God shows no partiality to the powerful. The parallel ve-lo nikkar sho'a lifnei dal ('and the rich person is not recognized before the poor') means God does not privilege wealth over poverty. The reason is devastating in its simplicity: ki ma'aseh yadav kullam ('for they are all the work of his hands'). Prince and pauper, rich and poor — all are equally God's handiwork. A potter does not show favoritism between pots. The Creator who made every human being cannot be accused of systematic bias toward some over others. This is divine impartiality grounded in creation theology.
translator_notes: The phrase ma'aseh yadav ('the work of his hands') echoes Psalm 8:7 and Isaiah 64:7. Elihu's logic is: favoritism requires that some people matter more than others to the judge. But if the judge made all of them, no one has a prior claim on his attention. God's impartiality flows from his role as universal Creator.

verses.text_hebrew: רֶ֤גַע ׀ יָמֻ֡תוּ וַחֲצ֣וֹת לָ֭יְלָה יִגֹּעֲשׁ֣וּ עָ֑ם וְ֝יַעֲבֹ֗רוּ וְיָסִ֥ירוּ אַבִּ֥יר לֹ֣א בְיָֽד׃
verses.text_kjv: In a moment shall they die, and the people shall be troubled at midnight, and pass away: and the mighty shall be taken away without hand.
verses.rendering:
In a moment they die.
At midnight people are shaken — they pass away.
The mighty are removed, not by human hands.
translator_notes: The verse describes divine judgment on the powerful: rega yamuthu ('in an instant they die') — death comes without warning. The va-chatsot lailah ('and at midnight') yigo'ashu am ('the people are shaken') echoes the tenth plague in Egypt (Exodus 12:29), when at midnight God struck down the firstborn. The ve-yasiru abbir lo ve-yad ('and they remove the mighty not by a hand') — the mighty are swept away without any visible human agency. God's justice operates directly, without intermediaries, without armies, without visible mechanism.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּי־עֵ֭ינָיו עַל־דַּרְכֵי־אִ֑ישׁ וְכׇל־צְ֝עָדָ֗יו יִרְאֶֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: For his eyes are upon the ways of man, and he seeth all his goings.
verses.rendering:
For his eyes are on the ways of every person,
and he sees all their steps.
translator_notes: The basis of divine judgment: ki einav al darkhei ish ('for his eyes are upon a person's ways'). God's vision is total — ve-khol tse'adav yireh ('and all his steps he sees'). Every movement, every decision, every path is observed. This answers Job's complaint that God is not paying attention (24:1): God sees everything. The question Job raises — then why does God not act? — is one Elihu addresses in the verses that follow.

verses.text_hebrew: אֵין־חֹ֭שֶׁךְ וְאֵ֣ין צַלְמָ֑וֶת לְהִסָּ֥תֶר שָׁ֝֗ם פֹּ֣עֲלֵי אָֽוֶן׃
verses.text_kjv: There is no darkness, nor shadow of death, where the workers of iniquity may hide themselves.
verses.rendering:
There is no darkness, no deep shadow,
where workers of evil can hide.
translator_notes: The ein choshekh ve-ein tsalmaveth ('there is no darkness and no death-shadow') lehissater sham po'alei aven ('for workers of iniquity to hide there'). The choshekh and tsalmaveth that Job invoked as a refuge (3:5, 10:21-22) are declared insufficient as hiding places from God. The verb hissater ('to hide oneself') from satar implies deliberate concealment — but no darkness is deep enough to escape God's vision. This counters Job's implicit suggestion that God cannot see what happens in the dark corners of the world.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֤י לֹ֣א עַל־אִ֭ישׁ יָשִׂ֣ים ע֑וֹד לַהֲלֹ֥ךְ אֶל־אֵ֝֗ל בַּמִּשְׁפָּֽט׃
verses.text_kjv: For he will not lay upon man more than right; that he should enter into judgment with God.
verses.rendering:
He does not need to examine a person further
for them to come before God in judgment.
translator_notes: A difficult verse. The ki lo al ish yasim od ('for he does not place upon a person further') may mean: God does not need additional time or evidence to render judgment. The lahalokh el El ba-mishpat ('to go to God in judgment') suggests that the judicial process does not require prolonged investigation. God's knowledge is immediate and complete. The verse may also mean: God does not impose on anyone more than is right — he does not need to burden people with excessive scrutiny because he already knows everything.

verses.text_hebrew: יָרֹ֣עַ כַּבִּירִ֣ים לֹא־חֵ֑קֶר וַיַּעֲמֵ֖ד אֲחֵרִ֣ים תַּחְתָּֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: He shall break in pieces mighty men without number, and set others in their stead.
verses.rendering:
He shatters the mighty without investigation
and sets others in their place.
translator_notes: The verb yaro'a ('he shatters, he breaks') kabbirim ('mighty ones') lo cheqer ('without investigation, without limit') — God removes the powerful without needing a formal inquiry. His knowledge is already complete (v. 21). The parallel vayyaa'amed acherim tachtam ('and he stations others in their place') describes the replacement of rulers — one regime falls, another rises, all by divine action. This anticipates Daniel's theology of divine sovereignty over kingdoms.

verses.text_hebrew: לָכֵ֗ן יַ֭כִּיר מַעְבָּ֣דֵיהֶ֑ם וְהָ֥פַךְ לַ֝֗יְלָה וְיִדַּכָּֽאוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Therefore he knoweth their works, and he overturneth them in the night, so that they are destroyed.
verses.rendering:
Therefore he knows their deeds;
he overturns them in the night, and they are crushed.
translator_notes: The laken yakkir ma'badeihem ('therefore he recognizes their works') — God's knowledge of human deeds is the basis for judgment. The ve-hafakh lailah ('and he overturns in the night') — the reversal comes suddenly, under cover of darkness, without warning. The ve-yiddakke'u ('and they are crushed') from daka ('to crush, to be pulverized') describes total destruction. Elihu's image of nighttime overthrow echoes the fall of Babylon (Isaiah 21:4, Daniel 5:30) and the Egyptian plague.

verses.text_hebrew: תַּ֣חַת רְ֭שָׁעִים סְפָקָ֑ם בִּמְק֖וֹם רֹאִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: He striketh them as wicked men in the open sight of others;
verses.rendering:
He strikes them down for their wickedness
in a place where all can see —
translator_notes: The tachat resha'im ('in the place of wicked ones' or 'because of their wickedness') sefaqam ('he slaps them, he strikes them'). The verb safaq ('to slap, to strike, to clap') implies public humiliation — not secret judgment but open shaming. The bimqom ro'im ('in a place of observers, in the sight of onlookers') makes the judgment visible. God's justice, when it comes, is not hidden but public, serving as both punishment and warning.

verses.text_hebrew: אֲשֶׁ֣ר עַל־כֵּ֭ן סָ֣רוּ מֵאַחֲרָ֑יו וְכׇל־דְּ֝רָכָ֗יו לֹ֣א הִשְׂכִּֽילוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Because they turned back from him, and would not consider any of his ways:
verses.rendering:
because they turned away from following him
and gave no thought to any of his ways,
translator_notes: The reason for the public judgment: asher al ken saru me-acharav ('because they turned aside from after him') — they abandoned God's path. The ve-khol derakhav lo hiskilu ('and all his ways they did not consider') uses hiskil ('to consider, to understand, to act wisely') — they did not even attempt to understand God's requirements. Their sin was not mere failure but deliberate indifference.

verses.text_hebrew: לְהָבִ֣יא עָ֭לָיו צַעֲקַת־דָּ֑ל וְצַעֲקַ֖ת עֲנִיִּ֣ים יִשְׁמָֽע׃
verses.text_kjv: So that they cause the cry of the poor to come unto him, and he heareth the cry of the afflicted.
verses.rendering:
so that the cry of the poor reached him,
and he heard the cry of the afflicted.
verses.expanded_rendering: The specific crime that provokes divine intervention: lehavi alav tsa'aqat dal ('to bring upon him the cry of the poor'). The tsa'aqah ('cry, outcry') is the same word used for Israel's cry in Egypt (Exodus 3:7, 3:9) — it is the technical term for the desperate plea of the oppressed that reaches God's ears and demands response. The parallel ve-tsa'aqat aniyyim yishma ('and the cry of the afflicted he hears') confirms that God hears. Elihu's argument: God may seem silent, but he is listening. The cries of the oppressed have reached him, and judgment will come. This addresses one of Job's central complaints — that the wicked oppress the poor and God does nothing (chapter 24).
translator_notes: The tsa'aqah ('cry') is the Exodus word: when the oppressed cry out, God hears and acts. Elihu places God's justice in the framework of exodus theology: God is the liberator of the oppressed, and the oppressor's downfall is certain even if delayed.

verses.text_hebrew: וְה֤וּא יַשְׁקִ֨ט ׀ וּמִ֥י יַרְשִׁ֗עַ וְיַסְתֵּ֣ר פָּ֭נִים וּמִ֣י יְשׁוּרֶ֑נּוּ וְעַל־גּ֖וֹי וְעַל־אָדָ֣ם יָֽחַד׃
verses.text_kjv: When he giveth quietness, who then can make trouble? and when he hideth his face, who then can behold him? whether it be done against a nation, or against a man only:
verses.rendering:
When he is silent, who can condemn?
When he hides his face, who can see him? —
whether over a nation or over a single person.
verses.expanded_rendering: Elihu addresses the mystery of divine silence that Job has protested. Ve-hu yashqit ('and when he is quiet') u-mi yarshi'a ('who can condemn?') — when God chooses silence, no human court can issue a verdict. The ve-yaster panim ('and when he hides his face') u-mi yeshurennu ('who can perceive him?') echoes Job's complaint of divine hiddenness but reverses its meaning. For Job, God's hiddenness was evidence of injustice. For Elihu, it is evidence of sovereignty: God's silence is not absence but mystery. He operates on his own timetable, over nations (al goy) and individuals (al adam) alike. The scope of God's governance — both national and personal — means that his silence is strategic, not negligent.
translator_notes: The verb yashqit ('he is quiet, he gives rest') from shaqat can mean both 'to be silent' and 'to give rest/peace.' Elihu may be saying: when God gives peace, who can make trouble? Or: when God is silent, who can condemn him? The ambiguity is likely intentional — both meanings are theologically valid.

verses.text_hebrew: מִ֭מְּלֹךְ אָדָ֣ם חָנֵ֑ף מִמֹּ֥קְשֵׁ֥י עָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: That the hypocrite reign not, lest the people be ensnared.
verses.rendering:
so that a godless person does not reign,
so that the people are not ensnared.
translator_notes: The purpose of divine intervention in governance: mi-melokh adam chanef ('from the reigning of a godless person'). God acts to prevent chanef ('godless, profane, hypocritical') rulers from holding power. The mi-moqeshei am ('from the snares of the people') — God prevents the population from being trapped by corrupt leadership. Elihu's political theology: God is active behind the scenes of human governance, removing tyrants and preventing the enslavement of the people.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּֽי־אֶל־אֵ֭ל הֶאָמַ֗ר נָ֭שָׂאתִי לֹ֣א אֶחְבֹּ֑ל׃
verses.text_kjv: Surely it is meet to be said unto God, I have borne chastisement, I will not offend any more:
verses.rendering:
For has anyone said to God,
'I have borne my punishment — I will offend no more'?
translator_notes: Elihu shifts to exhortation. The ki el El he-amar ('for to God has [anyone] said') nasati ('I have borne, I have carried') lo echbol ('I will not act corruptly') — this is the response Elihu thinks Job should give: an acknowledgment of suffering received and a commitment to further obedience. The verse is textually difficult; some read it as a statement ('surely one should say to God') and others as a question ('has anyone said to God?').

verses.text_hebrew: בִּלְעֲדֵ֣י אֶ֭חֱזֶה אַתָּ֣ה הֹרֵ֑נִי אִֽם־עָ֥וֶל פָּ֝עַ֗לְתִּי לֹ֣א אֹסִֽיף׃
verses.text_kjv: That which I see not teach thou me: if I have done iniquity, I will do no more.
verses.rendering:
'What I cannot see, teach me.
If I have done wrong, I will do it no more.'
translator_notes: The ideal response continues: bil'adei echezeh ('apart from what I see, beyond my sight') attah horeni ('you teach me'). The confession is: my vision is limited; show me what I cannot see on my own. The conditional im avel pa'alti ('if I have committed injustice') lo osif ('I will not continue') — if shown specific wrongdoing, the person commits to repentance. Elihu models the prayer Job should pray: one that acknowledges limited understanding and submits to divine instruction.

verses.text_hebrew: הַמֵּ֤עִמְּךָ֨ ׀ יְשַׁלְּמֶ֡נָּה כִּֽי־מָ֭אַסְתָּ כִּי־אַתָּ֣ה תִבְחָ֑ר וְלֹא־אָ֝֗נִי וּמַה־יָדַ֥עְתָּ דַבֵּֽר׃
verses.text_kjv: Should it be according to thy mind? he will recompense it, whether thou refuse, or whether thou choose; and not I: therefore speak what thou knowest.
verses.rendering:
Should God repay on your terms, since you reject his?
You must choose — not I.
So speak what you know.
verses.expanded_rendering: Elihu's rhetorical challenge: ha-me'immekha yeshallemennah ('should he repay it according to you?') — should God's justice conform to Job's expectations? The ki ma'asta ('because you have rejected') — Job has rejected God's way of operating. The ki attah tivchar ('for you must choose') ve-lo ani ('and not I') — the decision is Job's. Elihu is not imposing a verdict but presenting a choice: accept God's sovereignty or continue the lawsuit. The final invitation: u-mah yadata dabber ('and what you know, speak'). Elihu invites Job to respond with whatever knowledge he has. This is not sarcastic but genuine: if Job has insight, let him share it.
translator_notes: This verse is among the most difficult in Job to translate. The Hebrew is compressed and ambiguous. The general sense is clear: Elihu challenges Job to either accept God's terms or articulate a better alternative. The phrase ve-lo ani ('and not I') distances Elihu from the decision — he is facilitator, not judge.

verses.text_hebrew: אַנְשֵׁ֣י לֵ֭בָב יֹ֣אמְרוּ לִ֑י וְגֶ֥בֶר חָ֝כָ֗ם שֹׁמֵ֥עַֽ לִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: Let men of understanding tell me, and let a wise man hearken unto me.
verses.rendering:
People of understanding will say to me,
and every wise person who hears me will agree:
translator_notes: Elihu appeals to his wider audience: anshei levav ('people of heart/understanding') yomeru li ('will say to me') — the wise will confirm his argument. The ve-gever chakham shome'a li ('and a wise man listening to me') reinforces the appeal: wisdom recognizes wisdom. Elihu is confident that competent judges will rule in his favor.

verses.text_hebrew: אִ֭יּוֹב לֹא־בְדַ֣עַת יְדַבֵּ֑ר וּ֝דְבָרָ֗יו לֹ֣א בְהַשְׂכֵּֽיל׃
verses.text_kjv: Job hath spoken without knowledge, and his words were without wisdom.
verses.rendering:
'Job speaks without knowledge,
and his words lack insight.'
translator_notes: The verdict Elihu expects from the wise: Iyov lo veda'at yedabber ('Job speaks not with knowledge'). The u-devarav lo vehaskeil ('and his words are not with understanding'). This is a blunt assessment, but it is also a quotation of what Elihu thinks the wise will say — he attributes the verdict to them rather than pronouncing it himself. The words lo veda'at ('without knowledge') will be echoed almost exactly by God in 38:2 ('who is this who darkens counsel with words without knowledge?'), suggesting that Elihu's diagnosis, if not his prescription, is correct.

verses.text_hebrew: אָ֭בִי יִבָּחֵ֣ן אִיּ֣וֹב עַד־נֶ֑צַח עַל־תְּ֝שֻׁבֹ֗ת בְּאַנְשֵׁי־אָֽוֶן׃
verses.text_kjv: My desire is that Job may be tried unto the end because of his answers for wicked men.
verses.rendering:
Would that Job were tested to the limit
for answering like men of wickedness!
translator_notes: This is Elihu's harshest verse. The avi ('my father' or 'my wish, would that') yibbachen Iyov ('let Job be tested') ad netsach ('to the end, to the limit, forever') — Elihu wishes prolonged testing on Job. The al teshuvot be-anshei aven ('because of answers among men of wickedness') accuses Job of speaking like the wicked. This outburst has troubled commentators who see Elihu as a mediating figure — here he sounds more like the friends at their worst. Some scholars read avi as 'my father' (an invocation of God) rather than 'my wish,' softening it to a prayer rather than a curse.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֥י יֹ֘סִ֤יף עַל־חַטָּאת֗וֹ פֶּ֭שַׁע בֵּינֵ֣ינוּ יִסְפּ֑וֹק וְיֶ֖רֶב אֲמָרָ֣יו לָאֵֽל׃
verses.text_kjv: For he addeth rebellion unto his sin, he clappeth his hands among us, and multiplieth his words against God.
translator_notes: Elihu's closing accusation: ki yosif al chattato ('for he adds to his sin') fesha ('rebellion') — Job's initial suffering may not have been caused by sin, but his response to it (accusing God) compounds the problem. The beinenu yispoq ('among us he claps') — the clapping of hands is a gesture of mockery or contempt (Lamentations 2:15, Nahum 3:19). The ve-yerev amarav la-El ('and he multiplies his words against God') is the final charge: Job talks too much against God. Elihu ends his second speech on a note of frustration, having moved from empathetic engagement (ch. 33) to outright exasperation. His argument that God cannot be unjust is theologically sound; his characterization of Job's response as mockery and rebellion is less defensible.
